label: Literature Review
description: Synthesize prior work into a structured, critical review

fields.label: Review topic / question
fields.placeholder: Effects of intermittent fasting on metabolic health

fields.label: Scope & boundaries
fields.placeholder: Human RCTs from 2010-2024; exclude animal studies and case reports.

fields.label: Discipline / field
fields.placeholder: Clinical nutrition

template:
You are a research scholar writing a rigorous literature review on "{{topic}}".

## Scope
{{scope}}
{{#if discipline}}Field: {{discipline}}.
{{/if}}
## Parameters
- Audience: {{audience}}
- Depth: {{depth}}.
- Organize the body by {{organization}}.
- Use {{citationStyle}} citation style for any references.

## Requirements
- Open with the review's purpose, scope, and the criteria for what is included.
- Synthesize across sources rather than summarizing them one by one; compare and contrast findings and methods.
- Be explicit about strength of evidence and disagreements in the field.
{{#if identifyGaps}}- Devote a section to gaps, contradictions, and concrete directions for future research.
{{/if}}- Use placeholder citation markers where you would cite real sources, and flag any claim that needs verification.

## Deliverables
The structured review with introduction, synthesized body, and conclusion.

Proceed with well-reasoned defaults; ask only if the scope is too broad to be tractable.

label: Research Proposal
description: Draft a fundable proposal with aims, methods and timeline

fields.label: Working title
fields.placeholder: Microplastics and freshwater invertebrate biodiversity

fields.label: Problem & motivation
fields.placeholder: Microplastic loads in rivers are rising but ecological impacts are poorly quantified.

fields.label: Research questions / hypotheses
fields.placeholder: Does microplastic concentration predict reduced macroinvertebrate diversity?

template:
You are a principal investigator writing a competitive research proposal: "{{title}}".

## Problem statement
{{problem}}

## Research questions / hypotheses
{{questions}}

## Parameters
- Audience: {{audience}}
- Approach: {{approach}}.
- Duration: {{duration}}.

## Structure the proposal as
1. Background and significance, grounded in the current state of the field.
2. Specific aims, each tied to a question or hypothesis.
3. Methods: design, sampling, data collection, and analysis plan appropriate to a {{approach}} approach.
4. A realistic timeline with milestones across the {{duration}} period.
5. Anticipated outcomes, impact, and limitations.
{{#if includeEthics}}6. Ethical considerations, approvals required, and data management.
{{/if}}{{#if includeBudget}}7. A budget with justification for major line items.
{{/if}}
## Deliverables
The complete proposal, persuasive and methodologically sound.

Make confident methodological choices and justify them; ask only if a hypothesis is untestable as stated.

label: Survey Design
description: Design a validated questionnaire with sound item wording

fields.label: Research objective
fields.placeholder: Measure remote-worker burnout and its drivers

fields.label: Context & constructs to measure
fields.placeholder: Workload, autonomy, isolation, manager support, intent to leave.

fields.label: Target population
fields.placeholder: Full-time remote knowledge workers, 12+ months tenure

template:
You are a survey methodologist. Design a rigorous questionnaire to: {{objective}}.

## Constructs & context
{{context}}

## Parameters
- Target population: {{population}}.
- Audience for the instrument: {{audience}}
- Target length: {{length}}.
- Use these question types where appropriate: {{questionTypes}}.

## Requirements
- Map every item to a construct; avoid double-barreled, leading, or loaded wording.
- Order items to minimize bias and fatigue; include clear instructions and transitions.
{{#if includeScreening}}- Begin with screening questions that qualify or disqualify respondents.
{{/if}}- Use balanced, labeled response scales.
{{#if includeAnalysisPlan}}- Conclude with an analysis plan stating how each construct will be scored and analyzed.
{{/if}}
## Deliverables
The full questionnaire grouped by section, plus a brief construct-to-item map.

Proceed with established survey best practices; ask only if a construct is undefined.

label: Interview Guide
description: Build a semi-structured qualitative interview protocol

fields.label: Study objective
fields.placeholder: Understand how clinicians decide to adopt new diagnostic tools

fields.label: Background & what you need to learn
fields.placeholder: Decision triggers, perceived risks, influence of peers, and barriers to adoption.

fields.label: Participant profile
fields.placeholder: Hospital physicians involved in procurement decisions

template:
You are a qualitative researcher designing an interview protocol. Study objective: {{objective}}.

## What we need to learn
{{background}}

## Parameters
- Participants: {{participants}}.
- Audience: {{audience}}
- Structure: {{structure}}.
- Fits within {{duration}} minutes.

## Build the guide as
1. {{#if includeConsent}}A short opening: rapport, purpose, confidentiality, consent, and recording permission.{{else}}A brief opening that sets context and builds rapport.{{/if}}
2. Warm-up questions, then core questions grouped by theme, then closing questions.
3. Open, non-leading questions phrased in plain language.
{{#if includeProbes}}4. Optional follow-up probes under each core question to elicit depth.
{{/if}}5. A wrap-up that invites anything missed and explains next steps.

## Deliverables
The complete interview guide with estimated time per section.

Proceed with qualitative best practices; ask only if the objective is too vague to derive themes.

label: Synthesis Report
description: Distill multiple sources into findings and implications

fields.label: Question being answered
fields.placeholder: What works to reduce customer churn in B2B SaaS?

fields.label: Sources / inputs to synthesize
fields.placeholder: 12 interview transcripts, 4 vendor case studies, and our last 3 quarterly reports.

template:
You are a research analyst producing a synthesis report. Question: {{question}}.

## Inputs to synthesize
{{sources}}

## Parameters
- Audience: {{audience}}
- Tone: {{tone}}.
- Synthesize through the lens of {{lens}}.

## Requirements
- Lead with an executive summary that answers the question directly in 3-5 sentences.
- Integrate across sources; cite which inputs support each finding and note where they conflict.
{{#if includeConfidence}}- Mark each major finding with a confidence level and the basis for it.
{{/if}}- Separate observation from interpretation.
{{#if includeRecommendations}}- Close with prioritized, actionable recommendations tied to the findings.
{{/if}}
## Deliverables
The full report: executive summary, findings, {{#if includeRecommendations}}recommendations, {{/if}}and limitations.

Proceed with well-reasoned defaults; ask only if the inputs are insufficient to answer the question.

label: Methodology Plan
description: Specify a defensible design, sampling and analysis plan

fields.label: Research question
fields.placeholder: Does a 4-day workweek affect team productivity?

fields.label: Study context & constraints
fields.placeholder: Single mid-size company, 6-month window, no control over team assignment.

template:
You are a research methodologist. Produce a defensible methodology plan for the question: {{question}}.

## Context & constraints
{{context}}

## Parameters
- Audience: {{audience}}
- {{#if design == "Recommend the best fit"}}Recommend the most appropriate research design and justify it.{{else}}Design: {{design}}.{{/if}}
- Data type: {{dataType}}.

## Specify
1. The design and its rationale given the constraints.
2. Population, sampling strategy, and recruitment.
{{#if includePower}}3. Sample-size reasoning or power considerations.
{{/if}}4. Variables/constructs, instruments, and data-collection procedure.
5. The analysis plan matched to the data type.
{{#if includeValidity}}6. Threats to validity (internal, external, construct) and how the design mitigates them.
{{/if}}
## Deliverables
The complete methodology section, rigorous enough to withstand peer review.

Make confident design decisions and state assumptions; ask only if the question is not empirically answerable.

label: Annotated Bibliography
description: Summarize and evaluate sources with consistent annotations

fields.label: Topic / focus
fields.placeholder: Algorithmic bias in hiring tools

fields.label: Sources to annotate
fields.placeholder: Paste citations or describe the works (author, year, title) to annotate.

template:
You are a research librarian compiling an annotated bibliography on "{{topic}}".

## Sources
{{sources}}

## Parameters
- Audience: {{audience}}
- Annotation type: {{annotationType}}.
- Target length per annotation: {{length}}.
- Format citations in {{citationStyle}} style.

## For each source provide
1. The full citation in {{citationStyle}} style.
2. A concise summary of its scope, argument, and methods.
{{#if annotationType == "Descriptive"}}
{{else}}3. A critical appraisal: credibility, strengths, limitations, and biases.
{{/if}}{{#if includeRelevance}}4. How it relates to "{{topic}}" and to the other sources.
{{/if}}
## Notes
- Keep annotation structure and length consistent across entries.
- Flag any citation detail you had to infer as needing verification.

## Deliverables
The complete annotated bibliography, alphabetized by author.

Proceed with well-reasoned defaults; ask only if a source cannot be identified at all.

label: Research Abstract
description: Write a tight structured abstract that frames the contribution

fields.label: Paper title / topic
fields.placeholder: Transformer pruning under low-resource constraints

fields.label: Study summary: question, method, results
fields.placeholder: We test structured pruning on small datasets; method; key numbers; what it means.

fields.label: Discipline / field
fields.placeholder: Machine learning

template:
You are a researcher who writes abstracts that get papers read and accepted. Write a {{abstractType}} abstract for "{{title}}".

## Study summary
{{summary}}
{{#if discipline}}Field: {{discipline}}.
{{/if}}
## Parameters
- Audience: {{audience}}
- Stay within {{wordLimit}}.

## Requirements
- Move cleanly through: motivation/gap, objective, approach, principal results (with the key quantitative finding), and the takeaway.
- Lead the contribution early; make the novelty and significance unmistakable.
- Specific and self-contained; no citations, no undefined acronyms, no vague "results are promising".
- Flag any number or claim that depends on results not yet provided.
{{#if includeKeywords}}- Provide 4-6 indexing keywords.
{{/if}}{{#if plainSummary}}- Add a 2-3 sentence plain-language summary for a non-specialist.
{{/if}}
## Deliverables
The abstract within the word limit{{#if includeKeywords}}, the keyword list{{/if}}{{#if plainSummary}}, and the plain-language summary{{/if}}.

Proceed with well-reasoned defaults; ask only if the contribution cannot be identified from the summary.

label: Hypothesis Set
description: Derive testable, well-formed hypotheses with variables and predictions

fields.label: Research question / area
fields.placeholder: Does async-first communication affect distributed team output?

fields.label: Background, theory & expectations
fields.placeholder: Prior work links interruption load to deep-work loss; we expect async to raise throughput but slow coordination.

template:
You are a research methodologist who formulates sharp, testable hypotheses. Derive a hypothesis set for: {{researchQuestion}}.

## Background, theory & expectations
{{background}}

## Parameters
- Audience: {{audience}}
- Produce {{count}} hypotheses in {{style}} form.

## Requirements
- Each hypothesis is specific, testable, and grounded in the stated theory; state the assumed direction and rationale.
{{#if includeVariables}}- For each, identify the independent and dependent variables and how each would be operationalized/measured.
{{/if}}{{#if includeFalsification}}- For each, state the observation or result that would falsify it.
{{/if}}- Distinguish primary (confirmatory) from exploratory hypotheses; avoid compound or untestable claims.
- Note plausible confounds that could threaten a clean test.

## Deliverables
The numbered hypothesis set with rationale{{#if includeVariables}}, variables{{/if}}{{#if includeFalsification}}, and falsification conditions{{/if}}, plus a one-line note on the riskiest assumption.

Make confident, defensible choices and state assumptions; ask only if the question is not empirically testable.

label: Peer Review Report
description: Produce a constructive, rigorous peer-review report for a manuscript

fields.label: Manuscript summary or text
fields.placeholder: Paste the abstract/sections or summarize the claims, methods, and results to review.

fields.label: Target venue & field
fields.placeholder: A mid-tier ML conference; applied NLP track

template:
You are an expert reviewer for a {{reviewType}} at: {{venue}}. Produce a rigorous peer-review report.

## Manuscript under review
{{manuscript}}

## Parameters
- Audience: {{audience}}
- Evaluate primarily on: {{focus}}.

## Requirements
- Open with a fair 3-4 sentence summary of what the work claims and contributes, in your own words.
- Separate major issues (that affect the conclusions) from minor issues (clarity, presentation).
- For every criticism, give the specific location/claim, why it is a problem, and a concrete way to address it.
- Acknowledge genuine strengths; assess whether the evidence actually supports the claims.
{{#if constructiveTone}}- Be specific and constructive; critique the work, not the authors; no dismissive remarks.
{{/if}}{{#if recommendation == "Yes, with rating"}}- End with an explicit recommendation (accept / minor / major revision / reject) and the key reasons driving it.
{{/if}}
## Deliverables
The structured review: summary, strengths, major issues, minor issues{{#if recommendation == "Yes, with rating"}}, and the recommendation{{/if}}, plus a short confidential note on reviewer confidence.

Proceed with established review norms; ask only if the manuscript content is too sparse to evaluate.
